title: Picking the Right Database
intro: Your task manager needs a database to store tasks. Think of it like choosing between a filing cabinet and a junk drawer — the right structure makes all the difference. There are many options, and the wrong choice can cost you days of rework. Make the right call.

teach.title: SQL vs NoSQL: The Big Fork in the Road
teach.body: SQL databases (like PostgreSQL) store data in structured tables with rows and columns. NoSQL databases (like MongoDB) store data as flexible documents. For a task manager with clear data relationships — users have tasks, tasks have categories — SQL is usually the better fit.

teach.title: Filing Cabinet vs. Junk Drawer
teach.body: A SQL database is like a filing cabinet — everything has a labeled folder, and you can find exactly what you need in seconds. A NoSQL database is more like a junk drawer — you can toss anything in, but good luck finding that one receipt when tax season comes. For organized data like tasks, you want the filing cabinet.

teach.title: Task Data: SQL vs NoSQL
teach.code:
SQL (PostgreSQL):
  tasks table: id | title | status | due_date | user_id
  Clean, structured, easy to query and filter.

NoSQL (MongoDB):
  { title: '...', status: '...', due_date: '...', user: {...} }
  Flexible, but relationships get messy fast.
teach.annotation: When your data has clear structure and relationships (tasks belong to users), SQL keeps things clean. NoSQL shines when data shapes are unpredictable.

teach.body: Knowing how to pick the right database is what separates toy projects from real products. The task manager you're building uses the same database tech as apps generating thousands in monthly revenue.
For a task manager, you want a SQL database. Your data is structured (tasks, users, categories), you need reliable queries (filter by status, sort by date), and SQL handles this perfectly.

start.narrative: You're setting up the database for your task manager. Your app needs to store tasks with titles, statuses, due dates, and priorities. Tasks belong to users. You need filtering and sorting. What type of database do you choose?

choices.text: A SQL database like PostgreSQL — my data is structured and relational
A NoSQL database like MongoDB — I want flexibility
Just use localStorage in the browser — no server needed

sql-path.narrative: Good choice. PostgreSQL is reliable and perfect for structured data like tasks. You're ready to design the schema.

choices.text: Move on to schema design

nosql-path.narrative: You set up MongoDB and start building. Things work at first, but when you try to query 'all tasks for user X sorted by due date and filtered by status,' the query gets complicated. Joining user data with tasks requires extra work. What do you do?

choices.text: Switch to PostgreSQL now before you're in too deep
Keep going with MongoDB — you'll figure out the queries eventually

local-path.narrative: You build the whole app using localStorage. It works on your laptop, but the data disappears when the user clears their browser, there's no way to sync across devices, and your friend can't see their tasks on their phone. What now?

choices.text: Add a real database — you need server-side persistence
Keep localStorage and just tell users not to clear their browser data

schema.narrative: You're designing the tasks schema. You need columns for the basics — title, status, due_date, user_id — but also need to think about relationships and types. What does your schema look like?

choices.text: id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK to users(id), title TEXT, status task_status (enum), due_date TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
id INT, user_id INT, title VARCHAR, status VARCHAR, due_date VARCHAR — keep types loose so you can put anything in
id UUID PK, data JSONB — stuff every field into one JSON column for flexibility

hosting.narrative: Schema is ready. Now you need to decide where the database actually runs. What's your hosting approach?

choices.text: Use a managed service like Supabase or Neon — they run PostgreSQL with automatic backups, connection pooling, and a dashboard
Install PostgreSQL on a VPS you rent yourself for full control
Just run it locally on your laptop for now

backups.narrative: The database is hosted and live. Real users will store real tasks. How do you handle backups and schema changes safely?

choices.text: Enable daily point-in-time-recovery backups, and manage every schema change through versioned SQL migration files committed to Git
Export the database to a CSV every few weeks and apply schema changes by running ad-hoc SQL in the dashboard
Skip backups — you have no users yet, and you can always restore from your local copy

scaling.narrative: Six months in, you have 5,000 active users and the database CPU sits at 70% during peak hours. The list query is starting to slow down. What do you do?

choices.text: Profile the slow queries with EXPLAIN, add the missing indexes, and route reads through a connection pooler
Pay for the next compute tier — throw more CPU at it without changing anything in the code
Ignore it — 70% is fine, the database will sort itself out

excellent-end.narrative: Your database journey is complete: SQL for structure, a typed schema, managed hosting, point-in-time backups, versioned migrations, and a clean scaling path with indexes and pooling. Your task manager runs on rock-solid foundations and will keep up as users grow.
excellent-end.feedback: This is how senior engineers think about databases — not just 'pick one and go,' but a chain of decisions covering type, schema, hosting, durability, and scale. Each decision compounds: the right ones make the next problem easier; the wrong ones make every future change painful.

mediocre-end.narrative: Throwing more compute at the problem buys you a few months. The bill grows, but the underlying slow query is still slow — and the next time traffic spikes, you'll be back here with a bigger CPU and the same problem.
mediocre-end.feedback: Vertical scaling is the easy answer, but it hides the real bottleneck. Profile first, add indexes, and only scale hardware after you've fixed the queries. Otherwise you're paying to run inefficient code faster.

weak-end.narrative: 70% becomes 95% during the next launch. Queries time out. Users see error pages. You spend a panicked Saturday adding indexes and rotating credentials while customers churn.
weak-end.feedback: Database performance issues compound fast. By the time you 'feel' it on the dashboard, users are already feeling it in the app. Set up alerts, profile slow queries weekly, and treat 70% sustained CPU as a yellow light, not a green one.
